story.description: A clip of marine life in the ocean: majestic whale breaching in ocean waters
story.hook: Whales show why the tail slap matters.
story.lead: Whales aren't showing off when they breach. That's why they do it. Look closer—each splash leaves a temporary cooler pocket in the water, just long enough for the whale to cool down. Watch this one twice: the splash pattern changes depending on which side hits first. Did your textbook get this wrong too?
story.script: Whales aren't showing off when they breach. That's why they do it. Look closer—each splash leaves a temporary cooler pocket in the water, just long enough for the whale to cool down. Watch this one twice: the splash pattern changes depending on which side hits first. Did your textbook get this wrong too?

story.description: A clip of cold-climate animals: playful penguins swimming indoors
story.hook: Penguins carry tiny air pockets into the water.
story.lead: Penguins carry tiny air pockets into the water. Watch the feathers before the dive, because penguins trap air that can help with insulation and speed. When bubbles release, the water drag changes around the body. The smooth swim starts in the feather coat. Which polar trick next?

story.description: A clip related to natural science, biology, research, fossils, or discovery: a happy female botanist using a microscope
story.hook: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing.
story.lead: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing. Watch the cell boundary, because division only works after the internal instructions are copied and organized. Then the cell can split the material into two working lives. At this scale, reproduction is careful logistics. Which tiny process next?
story.script: Cells have to copy themselves before dividing. Watch the cell boundary, because division only works after the internal instructions are copied and organized. story.description: A clip of farm animals: horses on pasture
story.hook: Horses show why the face memory matters.
story.lead: Watch this jaw move sideways—that's how horses eat. Their teeth grind in a figure-eight because their jaws can't go straight up and down. That's why they toss hay instead of biting like we do. Did you know their molars are flatter than a cow's? Thought so.
story.script: Watch this jaw move sideways—that's how horses eat. Their teeth grind in a figure-eight because their jaws can't go straight up and down. That's why they toss hay instead of biting like we do. Did you know their molars are flatter than a cow's? Thought so.
